text: The surgeon creates pneumoperitoneum and flips up the greater omentum and transverse colon to identify the middle colic vessels. The transverse colon is dissected to expose the middle colic artery near its origin, which is then clipped and cut.

The surgeon continues dissection to expose the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, visible entering the superior mesenteric vein from the right side. The gastric branch of the gastrocolic trunk is clipped and cut, revealing the middle colic vein, which is also clipped and cut. Further dissection exposes the right gastroepiploic artery, which is preserved, followed by clipping and cutting the colic branch of the gastrocolic trunk.

The surgeon performs further dissection near the duodenal jejunal flexure to expose the inferior mesenteric vein. The origin of the inferior mesenteric vein is divided to facilitate the subsequent takedown of the splenic flexure. The preserved gastroepiploic artery and the inferior border of the pancreas are clearly visible.

The surgeon performs further dissection to release the duodenal jejunal flexure. The transverse colon is mobilized by taking down both the hepatic and splenic flexures. The gastrocolic ligament is divided to expose the hepatic flexure, which is then taken down. The Cattell-Braasch maneuver is executed to expose the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire right hemicolon by dissecting along the white line of Toldt. The remaining gastrocolic ligament is divided from the transverse mesocolon towards the left side. The dissection continues as the lesser sac is entered, revealing the posterior surface of the stomach, and progresses towards the left side of the colon.
The surgical specimen of the transverse colon is displayed, showing India ink tattooing marking the location of the stenosing tumor.
